hook: You already know how to compute exact distributions for small, well-behaved samples — but real data is messy and sample sizes are large, making exact formulas rare or impossibly complex. Asymptotic theory gives you a powerful workaround: as $n \to \infty$, nearly everything you care about becomes normal, and that single fact unlocks a universal toolkit for inference. How far can one limit theorem really take you?
concept: The unifying idea is that large samples tame complexity — regardless of the underlying distribution, key statistics converge to tractable limits, letting us build confidence intervals, test hypotheses, and propagate uncertainty through nonlinear functions using a common asymptotic framework (the 'large-sample approximation' regime anchored by the Central Limit Theorem and Fisher information).

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Derivation of Sampling Distributions via Integration
topic_roadmap.description: You will master the integration and change-of-variables techniques used to derive exact sampling distributions, building the foundation that makes asymptotic approximations meaningful by contrast.

You will prove and apply the result that the MLE $\hat{\theta}$ is approximately $\mathcal{N}\!\left(\theta,\, [nI(\theta)]^{-1}\right)$ for large $n$, connecting Fisher information to the precision of maximum likelihood estimates.

Apply the Delta Method to derive the asymptotic distribution of any smooth transformation $g(\hat{\theta})$, enabling uncertainty quantification for quantities like log-odds, variance components, or predicted probabilities.
You will construct ellipsoidal confidence regions for vector parameters and apply simultaneous inference corrections, controlling error rates when making multiple inferences at once.
